title: Orphan lamb bottle feeding
subtitle: A schedule and technique for raising a bottle lamb from day one.
excerpt: An orphan lamb, a triplet rejected by her mother, or a sickly lamb the ewe will not stand for needs bottle feeding from the first day. This walks through the colostrum priority window, the milk replacer, the feeding schedule, and how to wean to grass by twelve weeks.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb orphan lamb management notes. NADIS lamb-rearing parasites factsheet. SCOPS guidance on artificial-rearing parasite risk. SAC Consulting Scotland advisory papers on orphan lambs.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first thick yellow milk the ewe produces in the twelve hours after lambing. Carries the antibodies the lamb needs to fight off scours and pneumonia in the first weeks of life. Without colostrum in the first six hours, a lamb is at high risk of dying in the first week.
A powdered substitute for ewe's milk, mixed with warm water to feed orphan lambs. Specifically formulated for sheep; calf or cow-milk replacers are too low in protein and fat for lamb growth.

The first and largest stomach of a sheep or other ruminant, where fibrous plant material ferments. A lamb's rumen develops over the first six weeks of life as she begins to eat solid feed; until then she is functionally a single-stomached animal.

recipeTools.notes: For navel dip in the first hour.

content.text: Lambing throws up orphans, rejects, and weak triplets that the ewe will not stand for. A bottle lamb takes more work than a ewe-reared one, but a routine of warm milk on a steady schedule will see her through to grass and on to weaning. The first six hours are the critical window; after that the schedule is the simpler part.
Colostrum first
Colostrum

is the priority feed for the first twelve hours. Aim for at least fifty millilitres per kilo of body weight in the first six hours, and a further fifty per kilo by twelve hours. A four-kilo lamb needs two hundred millilitres in the first six hours and the same again in the second six. Without colostrum the lamb has no antibody cover and is at high risk of pneumonia, joint ill, and watery mouth in the first ten days.

First-choice source is ewe colostrum, milked off a quiet ewe whose own lamb has had her fill. Second-best is frozen colostrum kept in fifty-millilitre ice-cube blocks from previous lambings; thaw gently in warm water, never the microwave (heat destroys the antibodies). Third option is commercial powdered colostrum from the vet supply, mixed to the bottle instructions. Get the first feed in within an hour of birth if you can; the gut absorbs antibodies efficiently for the first six hours and inefficiently after that.
Switching to milk replacer
After the first twenty-four hours, switch the lamb to a sheep-specific
milk replacer

. Mix the powder with warm water at the rate on the bag (usually about two hundred grams of powder per litre of water). Feed at blood temperature, between thirty-eight and forty degrees; test by dripping on the wrist. Too cold and the lamb refuses; too hot and the gut burns.
The feeding schedule
Lambs do best on small frequent feeds in the first week, dropping back to fewer larger feeds as they grow. A working schedule:

Days one to three: four feeds a day, around 150 ml each, every six hours. Days four to seven: four feeds a day, 200 ml each. Week two: four feeds, 300 ml each. Week three onwards: three feeds a day, 400 ml each, dropping to two feeds of 500 ml from week six. By week eight she is on grass and solid feed; the bottle is offered once a day and dropped at week twelve.

A guideline is to feed about twenty per cent of body weight per day in milk in the first week, dropping toward fifteen per cent by week three. A four-kilo lamb on day one takes about eight hundred millilitres of milk in twenty-four hours, split across the four feeds. Weigh her once a week and adjust the volume.
Introducing solid feed

Offer a small handful of high-quality lamb creep pellet from day seven, in a shallow bowl. Add a wisp of soft hay alongside. The lamb will mouth them out of curiosity at first; by week three she is eating both with enthusiasm. The solid feed develops the
rumen
so she can move onto grass by week six. Fresh water in a shallow bowl from day seven onwards; lambs sip even before they need to drink much, and the habit forms early.
Housing and warmth

A newborn orphan lamb needs to be kept warm and dry, ideally in a small straw-bedded pen indoors for the first week, then a draught-free shed pen for weeks two through six. A heat lamp set above the pen at lamb height keeps the temperature around twenty degrees; lower the lamp if she shivers, raise it if she lies away from it. By week six she is on grass during the day and a sheltered pen at night; by week eight she lives out full-time in mild weather.
Weaning

Cut the bottle gradually rather than abruptly. Drop one feed every few days starting in week eight; by week twelve she is off milk entirely. A lamb weaned at twelve weeks on grass and solid feed is fully ruminant, no longer needs a daily routine, and joins the flock. Lambs weaned too early lose condition; lambs kept on the bottle too long become demanding and slow to move onto solid feed.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Bottle-lamb troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Most early problems are temperature, schedule, or scour.

items.symptom: Lamb refusing the bottle in the first day.
items.cause: Milk too cold, teat hole too small or too large, or the lamb is too weak to suck.
items.fix: Check milk is at blood temperature. Check the teat hole gives a slow steady drip when inverted. If the lamb is weak, give a stomach-tube feed of colostrum once, then try the bottle again; many weak lambs perk up after one tubed feed.

items.symptom: Yellow watery scour in the first week.
items.cause: Often E. coli, sometimes overfeeding, occasionally rotavirus.
items.fix: Cut the milk volume by a third for forty-eight hours. Offer warm electrolyte solution between feeds. Call the vet if the scour persists or if the lamb is dull and refusing the bottle. A scouring lamb dehydrates fast.

items.symptom: Lamb hot to the touch and reluctant to stand.
items.cause: Fever, possibly joint ill or pneumonia from poor colostrum cover.
items.fix: Take her to the vet today. Watery mouth is another likely diagnosis in the first three days. Note the colostrum schedule on the lamb record; future lambs from the same source need extra cover.

items.symptom: Lamb growing slowly compared to ewe-reared siblings.
items.cause: Underfeeding, dilute milk, or the lamb is small for her age.
items.fix: Re-measure the powder against the bag rate. Increase the volume by ten per cent and watch for two weeks. Most artificially-reared lambs reach normal weight by weaning but trail their ewe-reared siblings by two to three weeks throughout the rearing phase.

content.text: An orphan lamb takes about twenty minutes of attention four times a day in the first week, dropping to ten minutes twice a day by weaning. Most rear successfully if the first day is got right.
